transcript.presenting_concern: Interoceptive exposure for panic attacks in public settings

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Robert, good afternoon. How have things been since our last session where we discussed interoceptive exposure?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: It's been a mixed bag. I did the exercises you suggested — the straw breathing to simulate shortness of breath, spinning in the chair for dizziness. At home they were uncomfortable but manageable. But on Wednesday I had to go to a networking event for work and I almost lost it in the parking lot before I even went in.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Tell me more about what happened in the parking lot.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I parked and just sat there. My heart started racing, I could feel my palms getting sweaty, and the thought was "You're going to have a panic attack in front of all these people. They'll see you lose control. You'll embarrass yourself professionally." I sat in the car for fifteen minutes. I texted my wife and she talked me through it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And did you go in?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I did. I went in for about forty-five minutes. Had two conversations. Didn't have a full panic attack but I was probably at a 5 or 6 the whole time. I kept one hand in my pocket squeezing a stress ball.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: So let me reflect what I'm hearing. You experienced significant anticipatory anxiety in the car. You used supports — texting your wife. You entered the feared situation. You remained for forty-five minutes at moderate discomfort. And you did NOT have the catastrophe you predicted. Is that accurate?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Yeah. When you say it like that, it sounds like progress. In my head it felt like barely surviving.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: The "barely surviving" feeling makes sense because anxiety at a 5-6 for forty-five minutes is genuinely uncomfortable. But here's the critical learning: your brain predicted catastrophe — a visible panic attack, professional embarrassment, loss of control — and NONE of that happened. You were anxious and you functioned. That gap between prediction and outcome is exactly what we need your brain to register.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: But what if next time it IS a full panic attack? That's what keeps me from fully relaxing. The "what if."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That "what if" thinking is future-oriented anxiety. It keeps you in a state of threat anticipation even when the present moment is safe. Let's address it head on. Let's say you DID have a full panic attack at a networking event. Walk me through what would actually happen.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: I'd get really hot, my heart would be pounding, I might get that tunnel vision thing. I'd probably need to step outside. People would notice. They'd think something is wrong with me.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And then what?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: And then... I'd go outside, I'd do my breathing, it would pass. Like it always does. It usually peaks in about ten minutes.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Right. Even a full panic attack has a natural time limit. Your body cannot maintain that level of arousal indefinitely — the parasympathetic system kicks in and brings you back down. You've had panic attacks before. How many?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Probably... fifteen? Twenty? Over the last three years.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And in any of those twenty, were you permanently harmed? Did you actually lose control? Did you faint, collapse, have a cardiac event?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: No. My cardiologist cleared me. Nothing physically wrong. I just felt like I was dying each time.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: That's the cruel trick of panic — it creates the sensation of death or loss of control without the actual danger. After twenty panic attacks and zero catastrophes, your evidence base is strong: panic attacks are intensely uncomfortable but not dangerous. Today I want to push our interoceptive work further. Are you willing to try something in session?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: As long as I don't have to spin again. That one messed me up for an hour.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: [laughs] No spinning today. I want to try what's called a hyperventilation challenge. I'm going to ask you to deliberately overbreathe for about sixty seconds, which will bring on some of the physical sensations of panic — tingling, lightheadedness, chest tightness. The purpose is to practice experiencing those sensations without catastrophic interpretation. Ready?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Okay. Yeah. Let's try it.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Breathe rapidly and deeply through your mouth — like panting. I'll time sixty seconds. Start whenever you're ready.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Okay, stop. Return to normal breathing. What do you notice in your body?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Tingling in my hands. Light-headed. Chest feels tight. Heart is going fast. This is... this feels like the start of a panic attack.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Good. Now here's the key — you KNOW why these sensations are happening. You voluntarily produced them. They're not dangerous. Can you sit with them without reacting?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: [breathing] Yeah... it's uncomfortable but knowing I caused it makes it less scary. The tingling is fading already.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Rate your anxiety right now, 0-10.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Maybe a 4. It was a 6 during the breathing. But it's coming down fast because I know it's from the exercise.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: And THAT is the insight. These physical sensations — tingling, chest tightness, racing heart — they're not dangerous whether they come from hyperventilation in my office or from anxiety at a networking event. The sensations are the same. It's your INTERPRETATION of them that determines whether you panic or not.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: So it's not the physical feeling that's the problem. It's that my brain goes "oh god something is wrong" and then the fear of the feeling makes it worse.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Exactly. Fear of fear. That's the maintenance cycle in panic disorder. Sensation → catastrophic thought → more fear → more sensation → spiraling. Our job is to break that cycle at the interpretation stage. The sensation happens, and instead of "something is wrong, I'm going to lose control" you think...?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: "This is uncomfortable but it's just adrenaline. It will pass. I've been through this twenty times and nothing bad happened."

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Perfect coping statement. I want you to practice the hyperventilation exercise at home this week — twice per day for sixty seconds. Each time, sit with the sensations and practice your new interpretation. The goal is habituation — the sensations become boring rather than terrifying.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Twice a day. I can do that. It's weird that the homework is to make myself feel panicky on purpose.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It's counterintuitive but evidence-based. Avoidance maintains anxiety; exposure reduces it. By the next time we meet, those sensations should trigger significantly less fear because you'll have proven to yourself a dozen more times that they're harmless.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: And the networking event situation? Should I try another one?

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: Let's build up to it. For this week, I want you to do two things in addition to the interoceptive exercises. One: go to a coffee shop and stay for thirty minutes without your stress ball or texting your wife as an immediate support. Tolerate moderate discomfort independently. Two: write down three feared situations ranked by difficulty and we'll create a formal exposure hierarchy next session.

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Coffee shop I can do. The hierarchy sounds like it'll be anxiety-provoking just to write down.

utterances.speaker: CLINICIAN
utterances.text: It might be. That's okay. Bring whatever comes up and we'll work through it together. Same time next Thursday?

utterances.speaker: PATIENT
utterances.text: Same time. Thanks, Dr. Chen.

goals: Interoceptive exposure for panic attacks in public settings
